title: Move.toml pins pas/ptb to branch 'main', not a commit SHA
description: The pas and ptb dependencies are declared with rev = "main". Move tooling treats this as a mutable branch name. The Move.lock currently pins a specific SHA (b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc), but any lockfile regeneration (sui move update-deps, Move.lock deletion, or CI rebuild in a fresh workspace) will silently roll forward to the latest main commit. Upstream PAS is on testnet only and evolves — a breaking change in policy/send_funds/clawback_funds APIs can land without any Hadron-side signal, and the next build will compile against new semantics while reviewers believe the tested SHA still applies.
impact: Non-reproducible builds; silent API drift against upstream PAS can break compliance semantics (e.g. required_approvals, resolve order) without a CI signal.
recommendation: Change both rev values to the exact SHA currently in Move.lock (e.g. rev = "b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc"). Promote SHA updates to conscious bumps with a changelog entry and re-run integration tests.
evidence:
pas = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/pas", rev = "main" }
ptb = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/ptb", rev = "main" }

title: edition = "2024.beta" is out-of-date; should be "2024"
description: The package manifest still declares edition = "2024.beta". Sui Move has promoted the 2024 edition to stable; the token_template_pas/Move.toml in the same PR correctly uses edition = "2024". Mismatched edition strings across sibling packages is a drift hazard.
impact: Inconsistency across packages; potential warning/deprecation when tooling drops the beta tag.
recommendation: Change edition to "2024" in the root Move.toml to match token_template_pas/Move.toml and the stable edition string.
evidence: edition = "2024.beta"

title: treasury whitelist getters abort opaquely for non-PAS coins
description: Public functions is_whitelisted<T>, can_send<T>, and can_receive<T> call dynamic_object_field::borrow directly without first checking is_pas_coin<T>(). For a non-PAS coin type T (whitelist DOF absent), the borrow aborts with a framework-level dynamic_field abort code and no meaningful message. These functions are public and phantom-parameterized in T — any off-chain caller (SDK, indexer, explorer) or adversarial on-chain caller can trigger the abort for any arbitrary T and get an opaque failure. Worse, code inside pas_supply::mint indirectly reaches this via assert_can_receive — if a non-PAS coin slips into this call path (e.g. via a future refactor), the error surface is framework-level, not the intended ENotWhitelistedReceive.
impact: Opaque errors for integrators; tighter error contract is lost. A caller cannot distinguish "coin not registered as PAS" from "address not whitelisted".
recommendation: Add an explicit assert!(is_pas_coin<T>(treasury), ENotPasCoin) at the top of is_whitelisted, can_send, can_receive — or return false when the DOF is absent. For the assert-variants (assert_can_send / assert_can_receive), the guard should also run so the abort reason is actionable.

title: Whitelist mutators bound by is_coin_registered, not is_pas_coin
description: add_to_whitelist / remove_from_whitelist / set_whitelist_send / set_whitelist_receive require an Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>. In hadron::auth::new_auth, the check is assert!(treasury.is_coin_registered<T>()) — this is true for both PAS coins and regulated (non-PAS) coins. If a non-PAS coin T is registered via admin::register_coin, a holder of WhitelistManagePermission can successfully construct Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T> and then call add_to_whitelist<T>, which aborts deep inside dynamic_object_field::borrow_mut (whitelist DOF absent) with an opaque framework error rather than the intended ENotPasCoin.
impact: Ambiguous error surface, and the Auth/phantom-T contract does not express the real precondition. Adds friction and foot-guns for institutions that mix PAS and regulated coins.
recommendation: Add a treasury.is_pas_coin<T>() assertion at the top of add_to_whitelist / remove_from_whitelist / set_whitelist_send / set_whitelist_receive in treasury.move (public(package) side), or add it at the pas_admin entrypoints with the existing ENotPasCoin. Consider specializing the Auth constructor with an is_pas_coin check for PAS-specific permissions.

title: register_pas_asset does not early-abort when T is already PAS-registered
description: register_pas_asset calls policy::new_for_currency first, which aborts with EPolicyAlreadyExists if a namespace-level policy already exists for Balance<T>. Only later does add_pas_coin_caps check the local DOF (ECoinAlreadyRegistered). If an operator accidentally attempts a second registration, the abort comes from upstream PAS code with a non-Hadron error; the local surface is opaque. Also, a second registration consumes the caller's TreasuryCap<T> into the function and aborts, potentially losing it in the aborted transaction.
impact: Opaque errors and non-intuitive failure mode for double registration. Low risk because the operator can inspect the tx and re-submit, but the UX is poor for a gated admin action.
recommendation: Prepend an explicit assert!(!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>() && !treasury.is_coin_registered<T>(), ECoinAlreadyRegistered) before the upstream policy::new_for_currency call, so the abort happens with a Hadron-local error before any caps get moved.

title: register_pas_asset does not call version.check_is_valid()
description: register_pas_asset takes &Version but never calls version.check_is_valid(). The upstream TreasuryAuth<RegisterCoinPermission> (via auth::new_treasury_auth) does check version at creation, so in the golden path the check is present. However, with RegisterCoinPermission held in a role, a caller can construct TreasuryAuth once and, if it has drop only (it does), reuse is not possible across TXs — so golden path is safe. Still, the module signature implies a version gate that isn't enforced locally, and update_transfer_template has the same gap (version arg, unused in the function body itself). If the Auth lifecycle changes (e.g. future refactor reifies it as key+store), version-gating breaks silently.
impact: Fragile versioning discipline; a refactor that decouples auth creation from version check would silently bypass version for PAS registration.
recommendation: Add an explicit version.check_is_valid() at the top of register_pas_asset (and update_transfer_template). This mirrors the defensive pattern in treasury::create and pas_transfer::approve_transfer.

title: approve_transfer pauses transfers whenever mint/redeem paused — conflated pause semantics
description: approve_transfer gates on !treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>() using the EMintRedeemPaused error. This conflates two distinct pause semantics: the Hadron phase-1 design defines PauseMintRedeemPermission as an application-layer pause specifically for mint/redeem (not transfers), with a protocol-layer GlobalPausePermission (DenyCapV2) reserved for a full stop. Reusing the mint/redeem pause flag for transfer approval means compliance officers cannot pause mint/redeem without also freezing settlement flow for all holders, which is operationally counter-intuitive and may contradict spec intent from Notion (`PauseMintRedeemPermission` is scoped to mint/redeem).
impact: A pause intended to stop new issuance also blocks all peer-to-peer PAS transfers — possibly not the intended policy surface. Creates ambiguity between "I want to pause issuance while investigating" and "I want to freeze the entire coin."
recommendation: Introduce a distinct PAS transfer pause (e.g. TransferPausedKey<T> via a new Pause permission) or document explicitly that the mint/redeem pause also pauses transfers and is the intentional policy surface. If it must be unified, rename the pause to something non-operation-specific.

title: approve_transfer validates wallet address, not account ID — spec/doc gap
description: Upstream send_funds.move documents that sender/recipient are the wallet or object addresses, NOT the account addresses, and `resolve_balance` sends funds to recipient_account_id.to_address() (the PAS account shared object ID), not to recipient. Hadron's whitelist is keyed by wallet address (good — matches spec), but the mapping between wallet address and PAS account is not enforced here. Because PAS accounts are permission-less to create (pas::account::create), anyone can create an Account for any address; approve_transfer validates the wallet side, but the balance lands in the account object at object::id(account).to_address(). For a UID-owned account (object owner), the wallet address is a synthetic uid.to_inner().to_address() that must also appear on the whitelist. This is documented in lead #10 but not in code — operators must know this to whitelist object-owned accounts.
impact: Whitelist usability foot-gun when object accounts are involved. Not a security break — upstream account::deposit_balance goes to object::id(account) regardless — but a documentation gap.
recommendation: Add a module-level comment in pas_transfer.move documenting that 'sender'/'recipient' are wallet OR object addresses (quoting upstream) and that object-owned account whitelisting requires adding the object-synthetic address, not the account ID.

title: approve_transfer ignores sender_account_id / recipient_account_id signals
description: The SendFunds<T> struct exposes sender_account_id() and recipient_account_id() but Hadron's approve_transfer uses only sender()/recipient(). A hostile PTB constructor could present a well-formed send_funds request where sender (wallet) is whitelisted but recipient_account_id points to an account owned by a different (possibly adversarial) wallet. Upstream send_funds::resolve_balance deposits into recipient_account_id.to_address() — i.e. the account shared object — so the account's owner cannot silently redirect funds. However, this means the whitelist check on `recipient` (the wallet address) only validates the ostensible recipient, while the actual balance lands in whatever PAS Account object was referenced; if those two diverge (malicious caller passes a mismatched recipient/recipient_account_id pair), the wallet-address whitelist check can pass while funds end up at a different account.
impact: Depends on upstream invariants: account::send_balance internally sets recipient_account_id = namespace::account_address_from_id(namespace_id, to) — which is derived from `to` — so the pair is consistent. But unsafe_send_balance and direct request::new callers may not maintain this. If the upstream API exposes send_funds::new publicly (it is public(package) inside pas), the pair is safe. Surface as low because the upstream API currently enforces consistency.
recommendation: Add a cross-check assertion verifying that recipient_account_id is the derived account ID for the claimed recipient address: e.g. `assert!(namespace::account_address_from_id(namespace_id, recipient) == recipient_account_id.to_address(), ...)`. If not feasible without &Namespace arg, document the reliance explicitly in the module docstring.

title: No tests for any new PAS module
description: There is no tests/ directory in the Hadron package, and no #[test] or #[test_only] functions exist in the new modules pas_admin, pas_supply, or pas_transfer (verified by grep across sources/). All three modules depend on upstream PAS behaviour (policy creation, required_approvals matching, template command shape, request.approve order, clawback/send resolve semantics) that is not pinned by a test. Regression risk is concentrated in the exact places where upstream API drift is most damaging: set_required_approval approval-type key, register_transfer_template command shape, and Request.approve witness matching.
impact: Any upstream API drift goes undetected until full-setup.ts hits testnet. Internal regressions (e.g. pause-flag semantic change, auth.is_coin_registered vs is_pas_coin) are not unit-testable.
recommendation: Add a tests/pas_admin_tests.move, tests/pas_supply_tests.move, tests/pas_transfer_tests.move covering: (a) register_pas_asset happy path (asserts whitelist DOF created, policy_cap stored); (b) double-register aborts with ECoinAlreadyRegistered; (c) supply::mint aborts with EPasCoinNotAllowed when T is PAS; (d) approve_transfer aborts on unwhitelisted sender/recipient/zero amount/paused; (e) burn/seize witness type matches policy required_approvals (guards against ClawbackApproval<T> address drift on package upgrade).

title: PAS transfer template encodes current package address — stale after upgrade
description: register_transfer_template uses type_name::with_defining_ids<TransferApproval<T>>().address_string() to get the package address, which is the CURRENT Hadron package address at the time of registration. After a Hadron package upgrade, the defining-ids address is unchanged (defining-ids is immutable post-publish), but `approve_transfer` will exist in the *new* package version. Clients building PTBs off the template will still call the old package's approve_transfer, which (per version.move's VERSION constant) will become invalid after migrate(), aborting with EInvalidPackageVersion. update_transfer_template re-registers the template, but because with_defining_ids is stable, calling it again from the new package produces the SAME address. The template cannot be updated to point to the new package without changing to with_original_ids or hard-coding the new package address.
impact: After a Hadron package upgrade + migrate, all in-flight PAS transfers break until update_transfer_template is called — but update_transfer_template uses the same defining-ids address and cannot target the upgraded package.
recommendation: Either (a) use type_name::with_original_ids<T>() for the package id (this returns the latest package address if the type is tracked across upgrades — verify against ptb::ext_input, which uses with_original_ids), or (b) accept the package id as an argument to update_transfer_template so operators can explicitly re-target post-upgrade. Add a test that simulates package upgrade + template re-registration.

title: register_transfer_template uses with_defining_ids while ext_input uses with_original_ids
description: In the template command, the package_id is derived from with_defining_ids<TransferApproval<T>>(), while ptb::ext_input<PAS>(...) (upstream ptb.move L270-274) uses with_original_ids on the namespace. The approve_transfer template will be keyed off the PAS template-registration dispatch (which uses with_defining_ids per templates.move L35), but the off-chain resolver may compare or join paths that use original_ids. Inconsistency between defining_ids and original_ids can cause the SDK to fail to resolve ext_input tokens or to dispatch to the wrong module. This is not an immediate bug — upstream templates.move correctly uses with_defining_ids — but the mixed use of defining vs original is fragile across package upgrades.
impact: After an upgrade, off-chain PTB resolvers may key the template incorrectly if they use with_original_ids while the template is registered under with_defining_ids. Potential autoresolve breakage post-upgrade.
recommendation: Align the id form for TransferApproval<T> across all on-chain and documented off-chain paths. Document explicitly in a module doc-comment that template keys and Request approvals use with_defining_ids, and that the ext_input<PAS> namespace uses with_original_ids — so upgrade operators know what to verify.

title: PAS supply mint/burn/seize emits events after external calls — no reentrancy but ordering check
description: mint, burn, and seize all emit events at end after balance deposit / coin burn / resolve. Good pattern. However, approve_transfer emits PasTransferApproved *before* upstream resolve_balance is called (resolve is a separate SDK step). A re-subscriber counting emitted events as transfers completed could overcount if the subsequent resolve_balance never fires (caller aborts the PTB, though the emit is in the same PTB so effects are atomic — if the resolve aborts, the emit is also reverted). Net: correctness-safe under Sui's atomic PTB semantics, but document the coupling.
impact: None under Sui transaction atomicity. Informational only.
recommendation: Add a comment in pas_transfer.move clarifying that PasTransferApproved is emitted only within the same PTB as send_funds::resolve_balance. Subscribers should not treat this event as the final transfer — use the PAS-emitted settlement event if one exists.
evidence:
request.approve(TransferApproval<T>());
    events::emit_pas_transfer_approved<T>(sender, recipient, amount);

title: pas_supply::seize does not emit whether source is whitelisted — audit gap
description: seize emits PasSeized<T> with from/to/amount. The 'from' account's whitelist status (or de-whitelisted status) is not captured. A compliance audit tracing fund movements needs to know whether a seize hit a whitelisted or de-whitelisted account. The design-intent note in _context.md §7.2 explicitly says 'seize can extract funds from de-whitelisted addresses' — if so, the event should flag this.
impact: Audit/compliance tooling cannot distinguish 'seized from whitelisted account' (routine extraction) from 'seized from de-whitelisted account' (enforcement) from on-chain events alone.
recommendation: Add a `source_whitelisted: bool` field to PasSeized<T> (capture treasury.is_whitelisted<T>(from) before the resolve) — or emit two distinct event types. Minor but useful for downstream indexers.

title: pas_supply::burn / seize gate on is_mint_redeem_paused — unintended operational lockout
description: Both burn<T> and seize<T> assert !treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>(). Burn is the PAS equivalent of redeem — OK to pause. But seize is a compliance/enforcement action (e.g. suspend a sanctioned address). If compliance has paused mint/redeem (e.g. while investigating), they cannot seize funds from a malicious actor until they unpause — which is counter to operational intent. Usually an enforcement action should be available exactly when the system is paused.
impact: Operational lockout: pause + seize are mutually exclusive. In an incident-response scenario this can force a dangerous ordering (unpause to seize, then re-pause, with a window where the attacker can act).
recommendation: Drop the pause gate on seize (it's an admin/enforcement action). Keep it on mint and burn. Alternatively, document that seize is always allowed regardless of pause, and enforce a SeizePermission that is never held by the same role as pause to avoid unintended gating.

title: Single WhitelistManagePermission conflates add/remove — spec-drift from design
description: Per _context.md §7.2 (Notion spec drift), the original spec defined separate WhitelistAddPermission and WhitelistRemovePermission. Code collapses both to WhitelistManagePermission. This is a knowingly documented drift, but it removes the operational distinction between 'onboarding operator' (add-only) and 'exit/compliance officer' (remove-only). Least-privilege role design is lost.
impact: Cannot enforce separation between onboarding and offboarding roles; onboarding staff can also de-whitelist. In regulated deployments this blurs audit trails.
recommendation: Either (a) restore WhitelistAddPermission and WhitelistRemovePermission as separate permission types and wire through pas_admin, or (b) document in the module docstring that WhitelistManagePermission is intentional and explain the access-control trade-off.
evidence:
// ============ PAS Whitelist ============

public struct WhitelistManagePermission {}

title: keys.move doc comment for WhitelistKey is inaccurate (stores Table not bool)
description: The doc comment says 'DOF key for Table<address, bool> whitelist'. The actual stored value is Table<address, WhitelistEntry> (a struct with can_send / can_receive booleans), not Table<address, bool>. Minor but confusing for anyone auditing the DOF schema.
impact: Documentation drift — small.
recommendation: Update the comment to 'DOF key for Table<address, WhitelistEntry> whitelist (PAS assets)'.
evidence:
/// DOF key for Table<address, bool> whitelist (PAS assets).
public struct WhitelistKey<phantom T>() has copy, drop, store;

title: deployPasTokenTemplate hardcodes dependencies to 0x1, 0x2 — missing 0xC (coin_registry)
description: tx.publish declares dependencies: [normalizeSuiObjectId('0x1'), normalizeSuiObjectId('0x2')]. But token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move uses sui::coin_registry::new_currency_with_otw and MetadataCap — which live at 0x2 (sui-framework). The module itself is sui::coin_registry so 0x2 is correct. However, the new-style coin_registry requires the CoinRegistry shared object at 0xc to be passed at call-time (see full-setup scripts: COIN_REGISTRY = '0xc' is invoked separately in finalize_registration). The publish command itself may not need 0xc, but the deploy flow's deployAndRegisterPasAsset does reference 0xc hard-coded — which is a mainnet/testnet shared-object ID that can change. Confirm with the coin_registry upstream. Separately: normalizeSuiObjectId('0x1')/'0x2' is brittle; prefer SUI_FRAMEWORK_ADDRESS / MOVE_STDLIB_ADDRESS constants from @mysten/sui/utils for clarity and to catch typos at lint time.
impact: If the bytecode actually references coin_registry symbols defined in 0x2 at publish time, it works; but any shift to another package address or new dep at publish time silently breaks. Hardcoded 0xc is a cross-network assumption (testnet, mainnet, devnet all use 0xc for CoinRegistry? — verify).
recommendation: Replace '0x1' / '0x2' / '0xc' string literals with named constants from @mysten/sui/utils (SUI_FRAMEWORK_ADDRESS, MOVE_STDLIB_ADDRESS). Pull CoinRegistry shared-object ID from the network-specific deploy output, not a hardcoded 0xc.

title: deployAndRegisterPasAsset extracts object IDs via substring match — fragile
description: The deploy result is scanned with `t.includes('::coin::TreasuryCap<')` / `::coin_registry::MetadataCap<` / `::coin_registry::Currency<`. This is fragile: (a) matches any type whose fully-qualified name contains those substrings (unlikely but possible); (b) fails silently if Sui renames/versions coin_registry package; (c) breaks if multiple TreasuryCap objects appear in the same deploy output (the last one wins). The substring match also ignores the package address prefix — matches against any '::coin::TreasuryCap<' regardless of which package defined it (the sui framework one is the intended target, but nothing enforces that).
impact: A future change to Sui framework module names or a concurrent framework-level deployment could silently pick a wrong object ID and pass it to register_pas_asset, which would abort with a cryptic error.
recommendation: Prefer exact type-prefix matching against `${SUI_FRAMEWORK_ADDRESS}::coin::TreasuryCap<` and `${SUI_FRAMEWORK_ADDRESS}::coin_registry::MetadataCap<`. Or better: match by the Move resource's canonical type name (normalizeStructTag) instead of substring.

title: pas.ts pkg() may return '' with no guard — silent bad-tx
description: All operations (registerPasAsset, deployAndRegisterPasAsset, addToWhitelist, removeFromWhitelist, mintPasTokens) call `pkg()` — which falls back to '' if CONSTANTS.package.latest || v1 is empty (see constants.ts L63-65 and L53). If deploy-output.json is absent or parsed null, pkg() silently returns '' and the transaction is built against package '', which will fail at execution with an opaque effects error. No script-level guard asserts pkg() is non-empty.
impact: CI/dev pipeline gets stuck on invalid tx with opaque error rather than a clear 'run bun run deploy first' message.
recommendation: Add a guard at module load or at first call: `if (!pkg()) throw new Error('Package ID is not configured — run bun run deploy first')`. Alternatively, treat pkg() returning '' as an error in a helper and throw there.

title: mintPasTokens amount typed as `number` — u64 precision loss above 2^53
description: mintPasTokens has `amount: number`, and passes it directly to Move as a u64. JS numbers are IEEE-754 doubles, safe only up to 2^53 - 1 (~9.007e15). For a 6-decimal stablecoin, 2^53 base units is about 9 billion tokens — plausibly within operational bounds today but vulnerable if decimals increase or for low-decimals tokens. More importantly, the codegen-generated move call typically uses BCS serialization under the hood which may accept `bigint` or string; passing `number` is a latent foot-gun.
impact: For large mints (rare but possible), precision is lost silently and the on-chain amount differs from the intended amount.
recommendation: Change amount to `bigint | number` (accept both; coerce internally to bigint) and BCS-serialize via `bcs.u64()` explicitly. Same remediation for any future amount-bearing operations.

title: getBytecode.ts embeds raw hex — no provenance comment, no build-script regen
description: The file embeds BYTECODE_HEX as a single-line hex string with no comment explaining: (a) which token_template_pas commit it was compiled from; (b) how to regenerate it; (c) what Move compiler version was used. If token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move is edited, nothing ensures the bytecode is regenerated. This silently decouples the on-disk .move source from the hex actually published. Same pattern exists in the phase-1 deployTokenTemplate but the phase-2 reviewer should flag this as a new instance perpetuating the anti-pattern.
impact: Source/binary drift: a future edit to token_template.move (e.g. adding an event, changing decimals default) is not reflected in deploy unless someone manually regenerates the hex. High-risk for compliance-sensitive deployments.
recommendation: Either (a) add a script `bun run rebuild-pas-bytecode` that builds token_template_pas/ and writes the hex string; (b) add a CI check that diffs compiled bytecode against the embedded hex on PR and fails on drift; (c) at minimum add a comment block at the top of the file listing the source commit SHA and compilation command.

title: pas.ts uses `suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any` — silences type check
description: `new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any })` coerces the client to `any`. Likely because @mysten/pas expected a different Client type than SuiGrpcClient. This papers over a real type-system mismatch and will not catch future API changes in either SDK.
impact: If PASClient's expected client interface drifts (e.g. adds a required method), the cast hides the mismatch until runtime.
recommendation: Investigate whether @mysten/pas exports a type alias that accepts SuiGrpcClient or a SuiClient interface. If not, file an issue upstream and remove the `as any` once supported. Alternatively, adopt the PASClient client-factory pattern shown in full-setup.ts `client.$extend(pas())`.
evidence: const pasClient = new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any });

title: pas.ts mixes two PAS client construction patterns
description: operations/pas.ts uses `new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any })` (classic constructor), while examples/full-setup.ts uses `getClient(NETWORK).$extend(pas())` (plugin pattern). Two parallel patterns for the same SDK complicate maintenance and diverge on behaviour (e.g. caching, lifecycle). Standardize on one.
impact: Maintenance cost; potential subtle differences in plugin vs constructor behaviour go unnoticed.
recommendation: Pick one — the `$extend(pas())` pattern is the 2.0 SDK convention per Sui TS SDK docs. Migrate pas.ts to use `getClient().$extend(pas()).pas` and remove the `as any`.
evidence:
const client = getClient(NETWORK).$extend(pas());
    const pasClient = client.pas;

title: token_template_pas/Move.toml missing edition declaration check vs package name
description: token_template_pas/Move.toml declares edition = "2024" (correct for stable) while the top-level hadron Move.toml is still "2024.beta". They should agree. Also, [dependencies] is empty — ok because the bytecode is patched and the Sui framework deps are added at publish-time. However, the lack of an explicit Sui framework dep means `sui move build` run locally (to regenerate hex) will fail without an implicit framework resolution. Document this.
impact: Inconsistency across packages; source of confusion for contributors regenerating bytecode.
recommendation: Either (a) add explicit framework deps to token_template_pas/Move.toml so sui move build works standalone, or (b) add a README.md explaining the bytecode regeneration flow. Match edition string with root package.
evidence:
[package]
name = "token_template_pas"
edition = "2024"

[dependencies]

title: Potentially dangerous: permissionless approve_transfer exposes no rate-limit
description: approve_transfer is permissionless (anyone can submit a tx calling it, subject to whitelist checks) — this matches PAS design intent for autoresolve. However, the only rate-limit surface is the per-address whitelist bit (can_send / can_receive). There is no global throughput cap, no per-address per-period cap, no per-transfer amount cap. For a regulated stablecoin flow this may be insufficient for compliance rules that require e.g. 'no more than $X per day per address'. Spec §7.2 does not require this, so surface as info.
impact: Not a security bug. Informational — institutions considering PAS for KYC-gated but volume-capped flows will need additional off-chain or on-chain rails.
recommendation: If rate limits are in scope for a later phase, consider designing a `TransferLimits<T>` DOF (per-address, per-period) now to avoid an invasive refactor later. Out of scope for this PR; track as a future enhancement.

title: No DeepBook / display / standards-compliance audit trail for PAS coin deploy
description: The PAS token template deploys a non-regulated coin via coin_registry::new_currency_with_otw. Unlike the regulated template, no Display object is created, no kiosk/transfer policy is wired. Per Sui coin standards docs, coins typically also register Display for wallet rendering. Missing Display means PAS tokens may show as unnamed/iconless in wallets.
impact: UX impact only; PAS tokens may not render nicely in third-party wallets.
recommendation: Optionally wire a sui::display::new_with_fields call during deploy — or document explicitly that Display registration is out-of-scope and must be set up separately.

title: treasury.move pas fns use `let wl: &Table<address, WhitelistEntry>` at 4 sites — factor out
description: Six functions (is_whitelisted, can_send, can_receive, add_to_whitelist, remove_from_whitelist, set_whitelist_send, set_whitelist_receive) each re-fetch the Table<address, WhitelistEntry> via dynamic_object_field::borrow[_mut]. Factor out two helpers — `fun wl<T>(treasury: &Treasury): &Table<address, WhitelistEntry>` and `fun wl_mut<T>(treasury: &mut Treasury): &mut Table<address, WhitelistEntry>` — both of which assert is_pas_coin and return the Table. This would reduce duplication and enforce the is_pas_coin check centrally (also fixing R4-003 and R4-004 in one place).
impact: Code quality / maintainability. Reduces the attack surface of forgetting to add the is_pas_coin check to a newly-added whitelist operation.
recommendation: Introduce private `fun wl<T>(treasury: &Treasury)` and `fun wl_mut<T>(...)` helpers that (a) assert is_pas_coin and (b) return the Table reference. Refactor the 6 public functions to use them.
